headline: Welcome to the live coverage of stage 14 of the Tour de France 2026
subtext: The first contact with the Vosges was focused on the ascent and descent of Ballon d’Alsace, en route to Belfort, where attackers prevailed. Today is a proper rollercoaster with four major climbs and an elevation gain of 3,800 metres packed in155.3 km of racing between Mulhouse and Le Markstein Fellering. The peloton are set to roll at 13:10, to reach km 0 at 13:30. The winners will be crowned at around 17:30.

headline: Mulhouse, a classic of the Tour
subtext: Celebrated for its motor history, Mulhouse became a Tour stage town in 1925. Luxembourg’s Nicolas Frantz took his fourth stage win in that edition, his sixth in total, but Ottavio Bottecchia was head and shoulders above the competition in the overall standings. The Italian claimed his second Tour in a row six days later in Paris, while Frantz had to wait for the 1927 and 1928 editions to prevail. Today marks the 14th stage start from Mulhouse. The most recent, in 2019, gave way to a summit finish at La Planche des Belles Filles. Dylan Teuns won the stage while Giulio Ciccone took the Maillot Jaune from Julian Alaphilippe.

headline: Debruyne won't start
subtext: The Belgian rider from Alpecin-Deceuninck, who is 18th overall, 16’13” behind the Maillot Jaune, and 6th in the young riders' classification after breaking away in the stages to Foix (5th at the finish) and Le Lioran, will not be at the start this morning in Mulhouse due to a combination of “a sore throat and a fever”, according to his team.

headline: The battle for the white jersey is on
Thinking of Bartali and Clarke
subtext: A two-time winner of the Tour de France in 1938 and 1948, the legendary Gino Bartali (1914–2000) was born on 18 July in Ponte a Ema, near Florence. In addition to his two victories in the Tour de France, ‘Gino the Pious’ won the Giro d’Italia three times, as well as major races such as Milan – San Remo (four times) and Il Lombardia (three times). His record at the Tour de France includes two King of the Mountains titles, again in 1938 and 1948, as well as 12 stage victories spread across his eight appearances between 1937 and 1953. Simon Clarke was also born on 18 July, but in 1986. He won stage 5 of the Tour de France 2022in Wallers-Arenberg and retired at the beginning of 2026, after the Tour Down Under and the Cadel Evans Great Ocean Road Race.

headline: Let’s roll
subtext: The riders make their way to the start proper with a 12.5-km parade.

headline: Maillot Jaune
subtext: Tom Pidcock (Pinarello Q36.5) gained seven and a half minutes yesterday, moving up to the 4th place in GC, 4’15’’ behind race leader Tadej Pogacar (UAE Emirates-XRG) and just nine seconds behind third-placed Remco Evenepoel (Soudal Quick-Step). Jonas Vingegaard (Visma-Lease a Bike) is still second (+3’36’’).

headline: Green jersey
subtext: After a massive battle for the intermediate sprint, Mads Pedersen (Lidl-Trek) successfully defended his lead in the points standings. The Danish powerhouse has 377 points. Jasper Philipsen (Alpecin-Premier Tech) surpassed Biniam Girmay (NSN) yesterday: 336 points for the Belgian, against 333 for the Eritrean sprinter. Today’s stage offers up to 45 points: 25 at the intermediate sprint (Mélisey, km 137.8) and 20 at the finish.

headline: Polka-dot jersey
subtext: Tom Pidcock took 10 KOM points yesterday at the Ballon d’Alsace but Tadej Pogacar still leads the standings, with 42 points, and Jonas Vingegaard, who has 27 points, still wears the polka-dot jersey as the Slovenian star races with the Maillot Jaune. Today, there are 35 points up for grabs with three cat. 1 and one cat. 2 ascents.

headline: White jersey
subtext: Juan Ayuso (Lidl-Trek) retained the white jersey in Chalon-sur-Saône, with a gap of 13’’ to Paul Seixas (Decathlon CMA CGM) and 46’’ to Isaac del Toro (UAE Emirates-XRG).

headline: Be rivals like Tadej and Jonas
Team classification
subtext: Lidl-Trek still lead the standings but their margin against UAE Emirates-XRG is down to 9’18’’. Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe are back in third position overall (+36’58’’).

headline: Combativity award
subtext: Tom Pidcock (Pinarello Q36.5) races with a distinctive bib today as he was named the most combative rider yesterday. This honor also served as recognition of the efforts of his teammates Fred Wright, Xandro Meurisse and Quinten Hermans, all three of whom played a significative role in the progress of the breakaway. With this achievement, every team participating in the Tour 2026 has now been awarded at least once during its history on French roads.

headline: Into the final 1,000 kilometres of the Tour 2026
subtext: Believe it or not, we’ve already covered more than two-thirds of the Tour de France 2026. At km 1.7 of stage 14, there will be just 1,000 kilometres left in this 113th edition of the Grande Boucle.

headline: Jonas is ready for the challenge
First drops of the day
subtext: It is raining quite heavily on the route of the Tour de France 2026, 4 kilometers from the real start.

headline: Stage 14 of the Tour de France 2026 is ON!
subtext: The riders just passed km 0.

headline: 10 kilometres from the intermediate sprint
subtext: Alpecin-Premier Tech drive the bunch to try and ensure maximum points for Jasper Philipsen.

headline: The sun is out again
subtext: The riders have covered 5 kilometres and left behind the showers.

headline: Just over 5km from the intermediate sprint
2 kilometres to the intermediate sprint
subtext: Silvan Dillier (Alpecin-Premier Tech) is still driving the bunch.

headline: Philipsen wins the intermediate sprint
subtext: Alpecin-Premier Tech's Belgian fast man crossed the line ahead of Pedersen, Kanter and Girmay.

headline: Results of the intermediate sprint in Wattwiller (km 12.6)
subtext: 1. Philipsen, 25 pts 2. Pedersen, 20 pts 3. Kanter, 16 pts 4. Girmay, 14 pts 5. Stewart, 12 pts 6. Planckaert, 10 pts 7. Simmons, 9 pts 8. Teunissen, 8 pts 9. Marsman, 7 pts 10. Vacek, 6 pts 11. Askey, 5 pts 12. Armirail, 4 pts 13. Johannessen, 3 pts 14. Campenaerts, 2 pts 15. Denz, 1 pt

headline: The first climb of the day
subtext: Also known as the Ballon de Guebwiller, the Grand Ballon is the highest mountain in the Vosges, despite rising to just 1,424 metres above sea level. In this case, the riders will reach the summit at 1,336 metres above sea level, climbing from Uffholtz. This is the fourth time this mountain pass is tackled via a significant ascent in the Tour de France. However, it was also climbed in 2014 and 2019, preceded by the ascent to Le Markstein from Guebwiller, classified as a Category 3 climb; and in the Tour de France Femmes avec Zwift, on the 7th stage of 2022, from Willer-sur-Thur, and Annemiek van Vleuten claimed victory.

headline: Healy attacks early on the climb
subtext: The Irish attacker is followed by Tiberi, Foss, Hirschi, T. Johannessen...

headline: The Markstein seems to suit them well
A huge group on the move
subtext: Antonio Tiberi (Bahrain Victorious) and Tobias Johannessen (Uno-X Mobility) set the pace. We can see Marc Hirschi, Thymen Arensman, Richard Carapaz, Valentin Paret-Peintre, Quinn Simmons, Jan Tratnik, Bruno Armirail...

headline: The strong climbers who aren’t in contention for the general classification are already out in front
1 minute
subtext: UAE Emirates-XRG set the pace in the peloton behind a group of around 40 attackers.

headline: Tom Pidcock is once again in the breakaway
A first composition: Pidcock, Jorgenson, Carapaz...
subtext: Visma - Lease a Bike: Bruno Armirail, Victor Campenaerts, Matteo Jorgenson Red Bull-Bora-Hansgrohe: Mattia Cattaneo, Jan Tratnik Lidl-Trek: Quinn Simmons EF Education-EasyPost: Alex Baudin, Richard Carapaz, Ben Healy, Georg Steinhauser Decathlon CMA CGM: Aurélien Paret-Peintre Bahrain Victorious: Antonio Tiberi Netcompany Ineos: Thymen Arensman, Egan Bernal, Tobias Foss Soudal Quick-Step: Valentin Paret-Peintre Uno-X Mobility: Tobias Johannessen, Anders Johannessen Movistar: Pablo Castrillo, Einer Rubio, Raul García Pierna Pinarello-Q36.5: Tom Pidcock Groupama-FDJ United: Ewen Costiou NSN: Marco Frigo (NSN), Tudor: Marc Hirschi Caja Rural-Seguros RGA: Sebastian Berwick

headline: Attacks keep flying
subtext: With 12 kilometres to the summit, nine riders attack from the front group: Carapaz, Paret-Peintre, Tiberi, Arensman, Hirschi... Meanwhile, others close the gap form the peloton, including Van Eetvelt and Storer.

headline: An attack by Ben Healy, followed by Pablo Castrillo
Healy and Castrillo set off
subtext: They attack from the breakaway on a downhill section halfway up the climb. Meanwhile, Van Eetvelt bridges the gap to the chase group. His teammate Craps is also here.

headline: 30 kilometres into the stage
subtext: Healy and Castrillo are 10 seconds ahead of a group with around 30 riders. Higuita, Martin-Guyonnet and Pacher trail by 50 seconds. UAE Emirates XRG control the gap to the bunch: 1'25''.

headline: Healy and Castrillo managed to build up a lead
The final 5 kilometres on Grand Ballon
subtext: Ben Healy and Pablo Castrillo are reeled in by the chase group paced by Victor Campenaerts. The bunch trails by 1'30''. The'yve caught Martin-Guyonnet. Higuita and Pacher are just ahead.

headline: The two riders at the front have been caught by the chasing group
EF Education-EasyPost pull the breakaway
subtext: Georg Steinhauser currently sets the pace, with Ben Healy and then Ricahrd Carapaz on his wheel. The gap to the peloton decreases: 1'05''.
